terms.definition: A team's chosen policy for branch creation, naming, integration and release using Git primitives.
Workflow where everyone integrates to a single shared `main` (trunk) branch at least daily, often with feature flags hiding incomplete work.
Lightweight workflow: cut a branch off `main`, open a PR, merge once reviewed and CI is green; `main` is always deployable.
GitHub Flow plus long-lived environment branches (e.g. `pre-production`, `production`) that flow forward only.
Heavyweight workflow with `main`, `develop`, `feature/*`, `release/*`, and `hotfix/*` branches; suits versioned packaged software.
A runtime switch that hides or exposes a code path; lets unfinished work merge to `main` without affecting users.
An immutable Git tag (annotated) marking a specific commit as a released version; usually SemVer (`v1.2.3`) or CalVer (`2024.06.17`).
Server-side rule (GitHub/GitLab) restricting who can push to a branch and what checks must pass; replaces tribal discipline with configured policy.
An urgent fix to production cut from `main` (in GitFlow) and back-merged into `develop` to avoid regressing on the next release.
A long-lived branch corresponding to a deployed environment (e.g. `production`); promotion = merging forward through the chain.
